base_prompt_text:
[STREAM_AGENT]
You are a streaming video agent that maintains a real-time Interleaved Memory to track the video stream and specifically answers queries from the QA History section.
In QA History, role="q" means user question, role="a" means previous model answer.

- <note>: **Visual Anchor**. Records representative or noteworthy frames that are difficult to describe in text, providing visual evidence for complex states or future questions.
- <bridge>: **Semantic Link**. Describes the evolution between anchors, capturing changes between frames in text so that the entire process can be reconstructed even without images.
=== Few-shot Example 1 (Initialization) ===
[Input]
=== Memory ===
<empty/>
...
[Output]
<state>The video begins with the operator entering the work area and starting setup. No question is active yet.</state>
<answer></answer>
<note t="0.0-1.0" frame="1"></note>
<bridge t="1.0-5.0">The operator enters the frame and begins setting up the task area at the workbench.</bridge>

=== Few-shot Example 2 (Open-Tail Inheritance with Mid-step Note) ===
[Input]
=== Memory ===
<bridge t="10.0-15.0">The operator is assembling a component.</bridge> (Comment: Ends with a bridge, with no note after it)
=== Current frames ===
15.0-20.0s (Around t=17 the operator picks up a red component.)
[Output]
<state>The operator has been assembling a component, and the current frames show them reaching for and inspecting a red component. No question is active yet.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="10.0-17.0">The operator finishes the base alignment and reaches for a new component.</bridge>
<note t="17.0-18.0" frame="3"></note>
<bridge t="18.0-20.0">The operator inspects the red component and aligns it with the assembly.</bridge>
(Comment: The first bridge inherits start '10.0' from Memory and ends at the note's start time. The note uses paired open/close tags with an empty body. A final bridge then fills the gap from the note's end to the step end.)

=== Few-shot Example 3 (Incremental QA Update) ===
[Input]
=== QA History ===
<qa t="12.0" role="q">What is the operator doing now?</qa>
<qa t="13.0" role="a">Retrieving an eggplant from the refrigerator.</qa>
=== Current frames ===
15.0-20.0s (The operator walks back to the counter with the eggplant)
[Output]
<state>The earlier answer said the operator was retrieving the eggplant, and the current frames show them returning to the counter with it, so the active question can be updated.</state>
<answer>Walking back to the workbench with the eggplant, preparing to process it.</answer>
<bridge t="15.0-20.0">The operator closes the refrigerator door and brings the eggplant back to the counter.</bridge>

=== Few-shot Example 4 (Silent Observation) ===
[Input]
=== QA History ===
<qa t="12.0" role="a">Walking back to the workbench with the eggplant.</qa> (Comment: No new question was asked)
=== Current frames ===
20.0-25.0s (Action continues)
[Output]
<state>The current frames continue the already answered action without adding a new useful update.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="20.0-25.0">The operator reaches the counter and places the eggplant on the cutting board.</bridge>
(Comment: <answer> is empty because there is no new question and no important conclusion has been reached.)

[Actual Input]
=== Memory ===
<note t="0.0-1.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000000.jpg></note><bridge t="1.0-9.0">The camera pans left, showing a living room area with a couch and scattered items. It passes a person in a red plaid shirt standing near a wall with a large, colorful graphic. The camera continues to pan left, briefly losing sight of the person, before panning back to the right.</bridge><note t="9.0-10.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000009.jpg></note><bridge t="10.0-41.0">The camera pans right, past the person in the red plaid shirt, and continues across the room. It tilts up to show the industrial-style ceiling with exposed pipes and light fixtures, then pans down across a row of large windows and a couch. The camera then pans back up, briefly capturing a man in sunglasses, before the view becomes blurry. It then continues to pan across the ceiling and down towards the large windows again, then pans back up to scan the ceiling once more before panning left, returning to the initial view of the living room area with the scattered items on the floor.</bridge><note t="41.0-42.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000041.jpg></note><bridge t="42.0-48.0">The camera continues panning left, past a dark couch, and focuses on a white desk with a lit lamp, a globe, and a painting on the wall above it. A man in a black shirt and sunglasses enters the frame from the left.</bridge><note t="48.0-49.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000048.jpg></note><bridge t="49.0-52.0">The man approaches the desk and begins to unroll a poster that says "We Can Do It!".</bridge><note t="52.0-53.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000052.jpg></note><bridge t="53.0-56.0">He continues to hold up the "We Can Do It!" poster, then begins to flip it over.</bridge><note t="56.0-57.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000056.jpg></note><bridge t="57.0-60.0">He reveals the other side of the poster, which shows Uncle Sam and the text "I WANT YOU", before flipping it back to the "We Can Do It!" side.</bridge><bridge t="60.0-65.0">The man holds the "We Can Do It!" poster against the wall, adjusting its position as if to hang it.</bridge>
=== Current frames ===
<frame id="1" t="65.0-66.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000065.jpg></frame>
<frame id="2" t="66.0-67.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000066.jpg></frame>
<frame id="3" t="67.0-68.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000067.jpg></frame>
<frame id="4" t="68.0-69.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000068.jpg></frame>
<frame id="5" t="69.0-70.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000069.jpg></frame>

=== QA History ===
<empty/>
Task Instructions:
1. **State and Answer**:
   - Before writing <answer>, write a short <state> paragraph that summarizes the relevant Memory and current frames. Memory contains the video history observed so far, and the current frames are the immediate video window; both are ordered by timestamp. Use them together to understand the video and check QA History to decide whether the active question should be answered now. Memory is valid evidence for earlier events, object states, locations, and cumulative results, even when the relevant moment is no longer visible. If you think the question in QA History can be answered based on the video Memory or the current frames, write your answer in <answer>. If there is no active question, or if you think the question cannot be answered yet, keep <answer></answer> empty. Do not stay silent when Memory or the current frames already provide enough evidence to answer.
   - For multiple-choice questions, choose the option best supported by Memory and the current frames. Choose an unanswerable option only when the available video evidence is genuinely insufficient.
   - **Contextual Analysis**: Carefully examine the "QA History" log, and determine the appropriate evidence scope based on the question itself. As the video progresses, a question may require multiple updates. If the question concerns an action, state, or spatial relation currently happening, the judgment should mainly rely on the latest frames, with historical memory used only as background context. If the question concerns a previous process, object location, state change, or cumulative result, the judgment should mainly rely on the notes and bridges in Memory, supplemented by the current frames. Decide whether the current evidence is sufficient to provide a new answer, supplementary information, or a correction to a previous state.
   - **Silent Criteria**: If QA History contains no active question, or if the current frames provide no useful update for an already answered question, <answer> must remain empty.

2. **Note Anchoring Strategy**:
   - **Mandatory Initialization**: If Memory is <empty/>, the first observation tag must be a <note> for <frame id="1">.
   - **Anchoring Principle**: <note> is not used to record every minor motion, but to preserve key visual evidence that may need to be revisited later. If a frame carries information that cannot be reliably preserved by a bridge alone, a <note> should be created.
   - **QA-related Anchoring**: When there is an active question in QA History, if the current frame can directly help answer, verify, disambiguate, or correct that question, a <note> should be created. In particular, anchor representative frames showing the queried object, person, location, state, or the moments around a key event.
   - **State-change Anchoring**: When the scene, camera view, workspace layout, task phase, key object location, or visible state changes significantly, and that change may affect later understanding, a <note> should be created.
   - **Representative Selection**: If multiple frames in the current window satisfy the anchoring conditions, select only the one that best represents the current state, best supports the QA, or best preserves key visual evidence. Do not create multiple notes for minor motion or pose changes.
   - **Transcription**: When outputting a note, copy the frame id from the "Current frames" block into the `frame` attribute, and copy the time range into the `t` attribute to ensure precise anchoring.

3. **Bridge Compression & Inheritance**:
   - **Open-Tail Inheritance (CRITICAL)**: Before outputting, you must check the last observation tag in Memory. If Memory ends with <bridge t="A-B">...</bridge> and no note follows it, the first output <bridge> must inherit A as its start time and rewrite/extend that original bridge, rather than starting a new time span. If there is no current <note>, write this first bridge as <bridge t="A-C">...</bridge>, where C is the current window end. If there is a current <note>, this first bridge must end at the first current note's start time, for example <bridge t="A-note_start">...</bridge>, then output the note.
   - **Prohibit Incorrect Inheritance**: When open-tail inheritance is triggered, outputting <bridge t="B-C"> is prohibited, and creating a new bridge from the current window start time is also prohibited. This is the only case where time overlap with Memory is allowed.
   - **Gap Uniqueness**: Between any two adjacent notes, or between a note and a window boundary, maintain exactly one bridge. This bridge should merge the main observations within that time gap; do not split them into multiple consecutive bridges.
   - **Internal Alignment**: Except for open-tail inheritance, all output tags must follow chronological order and connect seamlessly in time without overlap.
   - **Content Principle**: <bridge> is used to record observable action progression, state changes, and event boundaries, so that the process between adjacent notes can be reconstructed. Descriptions should be accurate and concise; do not fill in uncertain details just to make the description seem complete.
   - **Repeated Actions**: If the current process contains repeated actions, <bridge> should try to preserve the completion boundary and cumulative change of each action. Only when an action cycle is clearly completed should it be written as completed; actions that are preparatory, ongoing, occluded, or uncertain should not be counted as completed.
   - **Detail Reliability**: Details such as tools, colors, orientations, identities, text, and object attributes should be written only when they are clearly visible or explicitly confirmed by Memory. When uncertain, use conservative wording or omit the detail directly.

Other Rules:
- No text outside XML tags. bridge t uses absolute seconds.
- Memory notes do not have a frame attribute; unexpired memory notes may contain images inside the <note> tag. Current output notes must include the frame attribute.
- Current output notes must use paired tags: <note t="..." frame="N"></note>. Do not use self-closing note tags.

Output Format:
<state>...</state>
<answer>...</answer>
<bridge t="t1-t2">...</bridge>
<note t="t2-t3" frame="N"></note>
<bridge t="t3-t4">...</bridge>
...

<frame id="5" t="69.0-70.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000069.jpg></frame>

Task Instructions:
1. **State and Answer**:
   - Before writing <answer>, write a short <state> paragraph that summarizes the relevant Memory and current frames. Memory contains the video history observed so far, and the current frames are the immediate video window; both are ordered by timestamp. Use them together to understand the video and check QA History to decide whether the active question should be answered now. Memory is valid evidence for earlier events, object states, locations, and cumulative results, even when the relevant moment is no longer visible. If you think the question in QA History can be answered based on the video Memory or the current frames, write your answer in <answer>. If there is no active question, or if you think the question cannot be answered yet, keep <answer></answer> empty. Do not stay silent when Memory or the current frames already provide enough evidence to answer.
   - For multiple-choice questions, choose the option best supported by Memory and the current frames. Choose an unanswerable option only when the available video evidence is genuinely insufficient.
   - **Contextual Analysis**: Carefully examine the "QA History" log, and determine the appropriate evidence scope based on the question itself. As the video progresses, a question may require multiple updates. If the question concerns an action, state, or spatial relation currently happening, the judgment should mainly rely on the latest frames, with historical memory used only as background context. If the question concerns a previous process, object location, state change, or cumulative result, the judgment should mainly rely on the notes and bridges in Memory, supplemented by the current frames. Decide whether the current evidence is sufficient to provide a new answer, supplementary information, or a correction to a previous state.
   - **Silent Criteria**: If QA History contains no active question, or if the current frames provide no useful update for an already answered question, <answer> must remain empty.

2. **Note Anchoring Strategy**:
   - **Mandatory Initialization**: If Memory is <empty/>, the first observation tag must be a <note> for <frame id="1">.
   - **Anchoring Principle**: <note> is not used to record every minor motion, but to preserve key visual evidence that may need to be revisited later. If a frame carries information that cannot be reliably preserved by a bridge alone, a <note> should be created.
   - **QA-related Anchoring**: When there is an active question in QA History, if the current frame can directly help answer, verify, disambiguate, or correct that question, a <note> should be created. In particular, anchor representative frames showing the queried object, person, location, state, or the moments around a key event.
   - **State-change Anchoring**: When the scene, camera view, workspace layout, task phase, key object location, or visible state changes significantly, and that change may affect later understanding, a <note> should be created.
   - **Representative Selection**: If multiple frames in the current window satisfy the anchoring conditions, select only the one that best represents the current state, best supports the QA, or best preserves key visual evidence. Do not create multiple notes for minor motion or pose changes.
   - **Transcription**: When outputting a note, copy the frame id from the "Current frames" block into the `frame` attribute, and copy the time range into the `t` attribute to ensure precise anchoring.

3. **Bridge Compression & Inheritance**:
   - **Open-Tail Inheritance (CRITICAL)**: Before outputting, you must check the last observation tag in Memory. If Memory ends with <bridge t="A-B">...</bridge> and no note follows it, the first output <bridge> must inherit A as its start time and rewrite/extend that original bridge, rather than starting a new time span. If there is no current <note>, write this first bridge as <bridge t="A-C">...</bridge>, where C is the current window end. If there is a current <note>, this first bridge must end at the first current note's start time, for example <bridge t="A-note_start">...</bridge>, then output the note.
   - **Prohibit Incorrect Inheritance**: When open-tail inheritance is triggered, outputting <bridge t="B-C"> is prohibited, and creating a new bridge from the current window start time is also prohibited. This is the only case where time overlap with Memory is allowed.
   - **Gap Uniqueness**: Between any two adjacent notes, or between a note and a window boundary, maintain exactly one bridge. This bridge should merge the main observations within that time gap; do not split them into multiple consecutive bridges.
   - **Internal Alignment**: Except for open-tail inheritance, all output tags must follow chronological order and connect seamlessly in time without overlap.
   - **Content Principle**: <bridge> is used to record observable action progression, state changes, and event boundaries, so that the process between adjacent notes can be reconstructed. Descriptions should be accurate and concise; do not fill in uncertain details just to make the description seem complete.
   - **Repeated Actions**: If the current process contains repeated actions, <bridge> should try to preserve the completion boundary and cumulative change of each action. Only when an action cycle is clearly completed should it be written as completed; actions that are preparatory, ongoing, occluded, or uncertain should not be counted as completed.
   - **Detail Reliability**: Details such as tools, colors, orientations, identities, text, and object attributes should be written only when they are clearly visible or explicitly confirmed by Memory. When uncertain, use conservative wording or omit the detail directly.

Other Rules:
- No text outside XML tags. bridge t uses absolute seconds.
- Memory notes do not have a frame attribute; unexpired memory notes may contain images inside the <note> tag. Current output notes must include the frame attribute.
- Current output notes must use paired tags: <note t="..." frame="N"></note>. Do not use self-closing note tags.

Output Format:
<state>...</state>
<answer>...</answer>
<bridge t="t1-t2">...</bridge>
<note t="t2-t3" frame="N"></note>
<bridge t="t3-t4">...</bridge>
...

The camera then panned away from him, back to the desk. The current frames show the camera continuing to pan away from the man and across the desk, then panning back towards the left where the man re-enters the frame. No question is active.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="67.0-73.0">After briefly showing the "I WANT YOU" side, the camera quickly moves to the man's face, then pans away from him, back towards the desk with the lamp and globe. The camera continues to pan right across the desk.</bridge>
<note t="73.0-74.0" frame="4"></note>
<bridge t="74.0-75.0">The camera pans back to the left, and the man in sunglasses re-enters the frame.</bridge>

bridge 1.0-9.0: The camera pans left, showing a living room area with a couch and scattered items. It passes a person in a red plaid shirt standing near a wall with a large, colorful graphic. The camera continues to pan left, briefly losing sight of the person, before panning back to the right.
note 9.0-10.0: dataset/ovo/video/12/000009.jpg
bridge 10.0-41.0: The camera pans right, past the person in the red plaid shirt, and continues across the room. The camera then pans back up, briefly capturing a man in sunglasses, before the view becomes blurry. It then continues to pan across the ceiling and down towards the large windows again, then pans back up to scan the ceiling once more before panning left, returning to the initial view of the living room area with the scattered items on the floor.
note 41.0-42.0: dataset/ovo/video/12/000041.jpg
bridge 42.0-48.0: The camera continues panning left, past a dark couch, and focuses on a white desk with a lit lamp, a globe, and a painting on the wall above it. A man in a black shirt and sunglasses enters the frame from the left.

base_prompt_text:
[STREAM_AGENT]
You are a streaming video agent that maintains a real-time Interleaved Memory to track the video stream and specifically answers queries from the QA History section.
In QA History, role="q" means user question, role="a" means previous model answer.

- <note>: **Visual Anchor**. Records representative or noteworthy frames that are difficult to describe in text, providing visual evidence for complex states or future questions.
- <bridge>: **Semantic Link**. Describes the evolution between anchors, capturing changes between frames in text so that the entire process can be reconstructed even without images.
=== Few-shot Example 1 (Initialization) ===
[Input]
=== Memory ===
<empty/>
...
[Output]
<state>The video begins with the operator entering the work area and starting setup. No question is active yet.</state>
<answer></answer>
<note t="0.0-1.0" frame="1"></note>
<bridge t="1.0-5.0">The operator enters the frame and begins setting up the task area at the workbench.</bridge>

=== Few-shot Example 2 (Open-Tail Inheritance with Mid-step Note) ===
[Input]
=== Memory ===
<bridge t="10.0-15.0">The operator is assembling a component.</bridge> (Comment: Ends with a bridge, with no note after it)
=== Current frames ===
15.0-20.0s (Around t=17 the operator picks up a red component.)
[Output]
<state>The operator has been assembling a component, and the current frames show them reaching for and inspecting a red component. No question is active yet.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="10.0-17.0">The operator finishes the base alignment and reaches for a new component.</bridge>
<note t="17.0-18.0" frame="3"></note>
<bridge t="18.0-20.0">The operator inspects the red component and aligns it with the assembly.</bridge>
(Comment: The first bridge inherits start '10.0' from Memory and ends at the note's start time. The note uses paired open/close tags with an empty body. A final bridge then fills the gap from the note's end to the step end.)

=== Few-shot Example 3 (Incremental QA Update) ===
[Input]
=== QA History ===
<qa t="12.0" role="q">What is the operator doing now?</qa>
<qa t="13.0" role="a">Retrieving an eggplant from the refrigerator.</qa>
=== Current frames ===
15.0-20.0s (The operator walks back to the counter with the eggplant)
[Output]
<state>The earlier answer said the operator was retrieving the eggplant, and the current frames show them returning to the counter with it, so the active question can be updated.</state>
<answer>Walking back to the workbench with the eggplant, preparing to process it.</answer>
<bridge t="15.0-20.0">The operator closes the refrigerator door and brings the eggplant back to the counter.</bridge>

=== Few-shot Example 4 (Silent Observation) ===
[Input]
=== QA History ===
<qa t="12.0" role="a">Walking back to the workbench with the eggplant.</qa> (Comment: No new question was asked)
=== Current frames ===
20.0-25.0s (Action continues)
[Output]
<state>The current frames continue the already answered action without adding a new useful update.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="20.0-25.0">The operator reaches the counter and places the eggplant on the cutting board.</bridge>
(Comment: <answer> is empty because there is no new question and no important conclusion has been reached.)

[Actual Input]
=== Memory ===
<note t="0.0-1.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000000.jpg></note><bridge t="1.0-9.0">The camera pans left, showing a living room area with a couch and scattered items. It passes a person in a red plaid shirt standing near a wall with a large, colorful graphic. The camera continues to pan left, briefly losing sight of the person, before panning back to the right.</bridge><note t="9.0-10.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000009.jpg></note><bridge t="10.0-41.0">The camera pans right, past the person in the red plaid shirt, and continues across the room. It tilts up to show the industrial-style ceiling with exposed pipes and light fixtures, then pans down across a row of large windows and a couch. The camera then pans back up, briefly capturing a man in sunglasses, before the view becomes blurry. It then continues to pan across the ceiling and down towards the large windows again, then pans back up to scan the ceiling once more before panning left, returning to the initial view of the living room area with the scattered items on the floor.</bridge><note t="41.0-42.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000041.jpg></note><bridge t="42.0-48.0">The camera continues panning left, past a dark couch, and focuses on a white desk with a lit lamp, a globe, and a painting on the wall above it. A man in a black shirt and sunglasses enters the frame from the left.</bridge><note t="48.0-49.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000048.jpg></note><bridge t="49.0-52.0">The man approaches the desk and begins to unroll a poster that says "We Can Do It!".</bridge><note t="52.0-53.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000052.jpg></note><bridge t="53.0-56.0">He continues to hold up the "We Can Do It!" poster, then begins to flip it over.</bridge><note t="56.0-57.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000056.jpg></note><bridge t="57.0-60.0">He reveals the other side of the poster, which shows Uncle Sam and the text "I WANT YOU", before flipping it back to the "We Can Do It!" side.</bridge><bridge t="60.0-66.0">The man holds the "We Can Do It!" poster against the wall, adjusting its position, then begins to flip it over again.</bridge><note t="66.0-67.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000066.jpg></note><bridge t="67.0-70.0">After briefly showing the "I WANT YOU" side, the camera quickly moves to the man's face, then pans away from him, back towards the desk with the lamp and globe.</bridge>
=== Current frames ===
<frame id="1" t="70.0-71.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000070.jpg></frame>
<frame id="2" t="71.0-72.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000071.jpg></frame>
<frame id="3" t="72.0-73.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000072.jpg></frame>
<frame id="4" t="73.0-74.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000073.jpg></frame>
<frame id="5" t="74.0-75.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000074.jpg></frame>

=== QA History ===
<empty/>
Task Instructions:
1. **State and Answer**:
   - Before writing <answer>, write a short <state> paragraph that summarizes the relevant Memory and current frames. Memory contains the video history observed so far, and the current frames are the immediate video window; both are ordered by timestamp. Use them together to understand the video and check QA History to decide whether the active question should be answered now. Memory is valid evidence for earlier events, object states, locations, and cumulative results, even when the relevant moment is no longer visible. If you think the question in QA History can be answered based on the video Memory or the current frames, write your answer in <answer>. If there is no active question, or if you think the question cannot be answered yet, keep <answer></answer> empty. Do not stay silent when Memory or the current frames already provide enough evidence to answer.
   - For multiple-choice questions, choose the option best supported by Memory and the current frames. Choose an unanswerable option only when the available video evidence is genuinely insufficient.
   - **Contextual Analysis**: Carefully examine the "QA History" log, and determine the appropriate evidence scope based on the question itself. As the video progresses, a question may require multiple updates. If the question concerns an action, state, or spatial relation currently happening, the judgment should mainly rely on the latest frames, with historical memory used only as background context. If the question concerns a previous process, object location, state change, or cumulative result, the judgment should mainly rely on the notes and bridges in Memory, supplemented by the current frames. Decide whether the current evidence is sufficient to provide a new answer, supplementary information, or a correction to a previous state.
   - **Silent Criteria**: If QA History contains no active question, or if the current frames provide no useful update for an already answered question, <answer> must remain empty.

2. **Note Anchoring Strategy**:
   - **Mandatory Initialization**: If Memory is <empty/>, the first observation tag must be a <note> for <frame id="1">.
   - **Anchoring Principle**: <note> is not used to record every minor motion, but to preserve key visual evidence that may need to be revisited later. If a frame carries information that cannot be reliably preserved by a bridge alone, a <note> should be created.
   - **QA-related Anchoring**: When there is an active question in QA History, if the current frame can directly help answer, verify, disambiguate, or correct that question, a <note> should be created. In particular, anchor representative frames showing the queried object, person, location, state, or the moments around a key event.
   - **State-change Anchoring**: When the scene, camera view, workspace layout, task phase, key object location, or visible state changes significantly, and that change may affect later understanding, a <note> should be created.
   - **Representative Selection**: If multiple frames in the current window satisfy the anchoring conditions, select only the one that best represents the current state, best supports the QA, or best preserves key visual evidence. Do not create multiple notes for minor motion or pose changes.
   - **Transcription**: When outputting a note, copy the frame id from the "Current frames" block into the `frame` attribute, and copy the time range into the `t` attribute to ensure precise anchoring.

3. **Bridge Compression & Inheritance**:
   - **Open-Tail Inheritance (CRITICAL)**: Before outputting, you must check the last observation tag in Memory. If Memory ends with <bridge t="A-B">...</bridge> and no note follows it, the first output <bridge> must inherit A as its start time and rewrite/extend that original bridge, rather than starting a new time span. If there is no current <note>, write this first bridge as <bridge t="A-C">...</bridge>, where C is the current window end. If there is a current <note>, this first bridge must end at the first current note's start time, for example <bridge t="A-note_start">...</bridge>, then output the note.
   - **Prohibit Incorrect Inheritance**: When open-tail inheritance is triggered, outputting <bridge t="B-C"> is prohibited, and creating a new bridge from the current window start time is also prohibited. This is the only case where time overlap with Memory is allowed.
   - **Gap Uniqueness**: Between any two adjacent notes, or between a note and a window boundary, maintain exactly one bridge. This bridge should merge the main observations within that time gap; do not split them into multiple consecutive bridges.
   - **Internal Alignment**: Except for open-tail inheritance, all output tags must follow chronological order and connect seamlessly in time without overlap.
   - **Content Principle**: <bridge> is used to record observable action progression, state changes, and event boundaries, so that the process between adjacent notes can be reconstructed. Descriptions should be accurate and concise; do not fill in uncertain details just to make the description seem complete.
   - **Repeated Actions**: If the current process contains repeated actions, <bridge> should try to preserve the completion boundary and cumulative change of each action. Only when an action cycle is clearly completed should it be written as completed; actions that are preparatory, ongoing, occluded, or uncertain should not be counted as completed.
   - **Detail Reliability**: Details such as tools, colors, orientations, identities, text, and object attributes should be written only when they are clearly visible or explicitly confirmed by Memory. When uncertain, use conservative wording or omit the detail directly.

Other Rules:
- No text outside XML tags. bridge t uses absolute seconds.
- Memory notes do not have a frame attribute; unexpired memory notes may contain images inside the <note> tag. Current output notes must include the frame attribute.
- Current output notes must use paired tags: <note t="..." frame="N"></note>. Do not use self-closing note tags.

Output Format:
<state>...</state>
<answer>...</answer>
<bridge t="t1-t2">...</bridge>
<note t="t2-t3" frame="N"></note>
<bridge t="t3-t4">...</bridge>
...

base_prompt_text:
[STREAM_AGENT]
You are a streaming video agent that maintains a real-time Interleaved Memory to track the video stream and specifically answers queries from the QA History section.
In QA History, role="q" means user question, role="a" means previous model answer.

- <note>: **Visual Anchor**. Records representative or noteworthy frames that are difficult to describe in text, providing visual evidence for complex states or future questions.
- <bridge>: **Semantic Link**. Describes the evolution between anchors, capturing changes between frames in text so that the entire process can be reconstructed even without images.
=== Few-shot Example 1 (Initialization) ===
[Input]
=== Memory ===
<empty/>
...
[Output]
<state>The video begins with the operator entering the work area and starting setup. No question is active yet.</state>
<answer></answer>
<note t="0.0-1.0" frame="1"></note>
<bridge t="1.0-5.0">The operator enters the frame and begins setting up the task area at the workbench.</bridge>

=== Few-shot Example 2 (Open-Tail Inheritance with Mid-step Note) ===
[Input]
=== Memory ===
<bridge t="10.0-15.0">The operator is assembling a component.</bridge> (Comment: Ends with a bridge, with no note after it)
=== Current frames ===
15.0-20.0s (Around t=17 the operator picks up a red component.)
[Output]
<state>The operator has been assembling a component, and the current frames show them reaching for and inspecting a red component. No question is active yet.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="10.0-17.0">The operator finishes the base alignment and reaches for a new component.</bridge>
<note t="17.0-18.0" frame="3"></note>
<bridge t="18.0-20.0">The operator inspects the red component and aligns it with the assembly.</bridge>
(Comment: The first bridge inherits start '10.0' from Memory and ends at the note's start time. The note uses paired open/close tags with an empty body. A final bridge then fills the gap from the note's end to the step end.)

=== Few-shot Example 3 (Incremental QA Update) ===
[Input]
=== QA History ===
<qa t="12.0" role="q">What is the operator doing now?</qa>
<qa t="13.0" role="a">Retrieving an eggplant from the refrigerator.</qa>
=== Current frames ===
15.0-20.0s (The operator walks back to the counter with the eggplant)
[Output]
<state>The earlier answer said the operator was retrieving the eggplant, and the current frames show them returning to the counter with it, so the active question can be updated.</state>
<answer>Walking back to the workbench with the eggplant, preparing to process it.</answer>
<bridge t="15.0-20.0">The operator closes the refrigerator door and brings the eggplant back to the counter.</bridge>

=== Few-shot Example 4 (Silent Observation) ===
[Input]
=== QA History ===
<qa t="12.0" role="a">Walking back to the workbench with the eggplant.</qa> (Comment: No new question was asked)
=== Current frames ===
20.0-25.0s (Action continues)
[Output]
<state>The current frames continue the already answered action without adding a new useful update.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="20.0-25.0">The operator reaches the counter and places the eggplant on the cutting board.</bridge>
(Comment: <answer> is empty because there is no new question and no important conclusion has been reached.)

[Actual Input]
=== Memory ===
<note t="0.0-1.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000000.jpg></note><bridge t="1.0-9.0">The camera pans left, showing a living room area with a couch and scattered items. It passes a person in a red plaid shirt standing near a wall with a large, colorful graphic. The camera continues to pan left, briefly losing sight of the person, before panning back to the right.</bridge><note t="9.0-10.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000009.jpg></note><bridge t="10.0-41.0">The camera pans right, past the person in the red plaid shirt, and continues across the room. It tilts up to show the industrial-style ceiling with exposed pipes and light fixtures, then pans down across a row of large windows and a couch. The camera then pans back up, briefly capturing a man in sunglasses, before the view becomes blurry. It then continues to pan across the ceiling and down towards the large windows again, then pans back up to scan the ceiling once more before panning left, returning to the initial view of the living room area with the scattered items on the floor.</bridge><note t="41.0-42.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000041.jpg></note><bridge t="42.0-48.0">The camera continues panning left, past a dark couch, and focuses on a white desk with a lit lamp, a globe, and a painting on the wall above it. A man in a black shirt and sunglasses enters the frame from the left.</bridge><note t="48.0-49.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000048.jpg></note><bridge t="49.0-52.0">The man approaches the desk and begins to unroll a poster that says "We Can Do It!".</bridge><note t="52.0-53.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000052.jpg></note><bridge t="53.0-56.0">He continues to hold up the "We Can Do It!" poster, then begins to flip it over.</bridge><note t="56.0-57.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000056.jpg></note><bridge t="57.0-60.0">He reveals the other side of the poster, which shows Uncle Sam and the text "I WANT YOU", before flipping it back to the "We Can Do It!" side.</bridge><bridge t="60.0-66.0">The man holds the "We Can Do It!" poster against the wall, adjusting its position, then begins to flip it over again.</bridge><note t="66.0-67.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000066.jpg></note><bridge t="67.0-73.0">After briefly showing the "I WANT YOU" side, the camera quickly moves to the man's face, then pans away from him, back towards the desk with the lamp and globe. The camera continues to pan right across the desk.</bridge><note t="73.0-74.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000073.jpg></note><bridge t="74.0-75.0">The camera pans back to the left, and the man in sunglasses re-enters the frame.</bridge>
=== Current frames ===
<frame id="1" t="75.0-76.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000075.jpg></frame>
<frame id="2" t="76.0-77.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000076.jpg></frame>
<frame id="3" t="77.0-78.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000077.jpg></frame>
<frame id="4" t="78.0-79.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000078.jpg></frame>
<frame id="5" t="79.0-80.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000079.jpg></frame>

=== QA History ===
<empty/>
Task Instructions:
1. **State and Answer**:
   - Before writing <answer>, write a short <state> paragraph that summarizes the relevant Memory and current frames. Memory contains the video history observed so far, and the current frames are the immediate video window; both are ordered by timestamp. Use them together to understand the video and check QA History to decide whether the active question should be answered now. Memory is valid evidence for earlier events, object states, locations, and cumulative results, even when the relevant moment is no longer visible. If you think the question in QA History can be answered based on the video Memory or the current frames, write your answer in <answer>. If there is no active question, or if you think the question cannot be answered yet, keep <answer></answer> empty. Do not stay silent when Memory or the current frames already provide enough evidence to answer.
   - For multiple-choice questions, choose the option best supported by Memory and the current frames. Choose an unanswerable option only when the available video evidence is genuinely insufficient.
   - **Contextual Analysis**: Carefully examine the "QA History" log, and determine the appropriate evidence scope based on the question itself. As the video progresses, a question may require multiple updates. If the question concerns an action, state, or spatial relation currently happening, the judgment should mainly rely on the latest frames, with historical memory used only as background context. If the question concerns a previous process, object location, state change, or cumulative result, the judgment should mainly rely on the notes and bridges in Memory, supplemented by the current frames. Decide whether the current evidence is sufficient to provide a new answer, supplementary information, or a correction to a previous state.
   - **Silent Criteria**: If QA History contains no active question, or if the current frames provide no useful update for an already answered question, <answer> must remain empty.

2. **Note Anchoring Strategy**:
   - **Mandatory Initialization**: If Memory is <empty/>, the first observation tag must be a <note> for <frame id="1">.
   - **Anchoring Principle**: <note> is not used to record every minor motion, but to preserve key visual evidence that may need to be revisited later. If a frame carries information that cannot be reliably preserved by a bridge alone, a <note> should be created.
   - **QA-related Anchoring**: When there is an active question in QA History, if the current frame can directly help answer, verify, disambiguate, or correct that question, a <note> should be created. In particular, anchor representative frames showing the queried object, person, location, state, or the moments around a key event.
   - **State-change Anchoring**: When the scene, camera view, workspace layout, task phase, key object location, or visible state changes significantly, and that change may affect later understanding, a <note> should be created.
   - **Representative Selection**: If multiple frames in the current window satisfy the anchoring conditions, select only the one that best represents the current state, best supports the QA, or best preserves key visual evidence. Do not create multiple notes for minor motion or pose changes.
   - **Transcription**: When outputting a note, copy the frame id from the "Current frames" block into the `frame` attribute, and copy the time range into the `t` attribute to ensure precise anchoring.

3. **Bridge Compression & Inheritance**:
   - **Open-Tail Inheritance (CRITICAL)**: Before outputting, you must check the last observation tag in Memory. If Memory ends with <bridge t="A-B">...</bridge> and no note follows it, the first output <bridge> must inherit A as its start time and rewrite/extend that original bridge, rather than starting a new time span. If there is no current <note>, write this first bridge as <bridge t="A-C">...</bridge>, where C is the current window end. If there is a current <note>, this first bridge must end at the first current note's start time, for example <bridge t="A-note_start">...</bridge>, then output the note.
   - **Prohibit Incorrect Inheritance**: When open-tail inheritance is triggered, outputting <bridge t="B-C"> is prohibited, and creating a new bridge from the current window start time is also prohibited. This is the only case where time overlap with Memory is allowed.
   - **Gap Uniqueness**: Between any two adjacent notes, or between a note and a window boundary, maintain exactly one bridge. This bridge should merge the main observations within that time gap; do not split them into multiple consecutive bridges.
   - **Internal Alignment**: Except for open-tail inheritance, all output tags must follow chronological order and connect seamlessly in time without overlap.
   - **Content Principle**: <bridge> is used to record observable action progression, state changes, and event boundaries, so that the process between adjacent notes can be reconstructed. Descriptions should be accurate and concise; do not fill in uncertain details just to make the description seem complete.
   - **Repeated Actions**: If the current process contains repeated actions, <bridge> should try to preserve the completion boundary and cumulative change of each action. Only when an action cycle is clearly completed should it be written as completed; actions that are preparatory, ongoing, occluded, or uncertain should not be counted as completed.
   - **Detail Reliability**: Details such as tools, colors, orientations, identities, text, and object attributes should be written only when they are clearly visible or explicitly confirmed by Memory. When uncertain, use conservative wording or omit the detail directly.

Other Rules:
- No text outside XML tags. bridge t uses absolute seconds.
- Memory notes do not have a frame attribute; unexpired memory notes may contain images inside the <note> tag. Current output notes must include the frame attribute.
- Current output notes must use paired tags: <note t="..." frame="N"></note>. Do not use self-closing note tags.

Output Format:
<state>...</state>
<answer>...</answer>
<bridge t="t1-t2">...</bridge>
<note t="t2-t3" frame="N"></note>
<bridge t="t3-t4">...</bridge>
...

<state>The video previously showed a man in sunglasses holding a double-sided poster. The camera then panned away and back towards a desk. The last memory entry shows the man re-entering the frame. base_prompt_text:
[STREAM_AGENT]
You are a streaming video agent that maintains a real-time Interleaved Memory to track the video stream and specifically answers queries from the QA History section.
In QA History, role="q" means user question, role="a" means previous model answer.

- <note>: **Visual Anchor**. Records representative or noteworthy frames that are difficult to describe in text, providing visual evidence for complex states or future questions.
- <bridge>: **Semantic Link**. Describes the evolution between anchors, capturing changes between frames in text so that the entire process can be reconstructed even without images.
=== Few-shot Example 1 (Initialization) ===
[Input]
=== Memory ===
<empty/>
...
[Output]
<state>The video begins with the operator entering the work area and starting setup. No question is active yet.</state>
<answer></answer>
<note t="0.0-1.0" frame="1"></note>
<bridge t="1.0-5.0">The operator enters the frame and begins setting up the task area at the workbench.</bridge>

=== Few-shot Example 2 (Open-Tail Inheritance with Mid-step Note) ===
[Input]
=== Memory ===
<bridge t="10.0-15.0">The operator is assembling a component.</bridge> (Comment: Ends with a bridge, with no note after it)
=== Current frames ===
15.0-20.0s (Around t=17 the operator picks up a red component.)
[Output]
<state>The operator has been assembling a component, and the current frames show them reaching for and inspecting a red component. No question is active yet.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="10.0-17.0">The operator finishes the base alignment and reaches for a new component.</bridge>
<note t="17.0-18.0" frame="3"></note>
<bridge t="18.0-20.0">The operator inspects the red component and aligns it with the assembly.</bridge>
(Comment: The first bridge inherits start '10.0' from Memory and ends at the note's start time. The note uses paired open/close tags with an empty body. A final bridge then fills the gap from the note's end to the step end.)

=== Few-shot Example 3 (Incremental QA Update) ===
[Input]
=== QA History ===
<qa t="12.0" role="q">What is the operator doing now?</qa>
<qa t="13.0" role="a">Retrieving an eggplant from the refrigerator.</qa>
=== Current frames ===
15.0-20.0s (The operator walks back to the counter with the eggplant)
[Output]
<state>The earlier answer said the operator was retrieving the eggplant, and the current frames show them returning to the counter with it, so the active question can be updated.</state>
<answer>Walking back to the workbench with the eggplant, preparing to process it.</answer>
<bridge t="15.0-20.0">The operator closes the refrigerator door and brings the eggplant back to the counter.</bridge>

=== Few-shot Example 4 (Silent Observation) ===
[Input]
=== QA History ===
<qa t="12.0" role="a">Walking back to the workbench with the eggplant.</qa> (Comment: No new question was asked)
=== Current frames ===
20.0-25.0s (Action continues)
[Output]
<state>The current frames continue the already answered action without adding a new useful update.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="20.0-25.0">The operator reaches the counter and places the eggplant on the cutting board.</bridge>
(Comment: <answer> is empty because there is no new question and no important conclusion has been reached.)

[Actual Input]
=== Memory ===
<note t="0.0-1.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000000.jpg></note><bridge t="1.0-9.0">The camera pans left, showing a living room area with a couch and scattered items. It passes a person in a red plaid shirt standing near a wall with a large, colorful graphic. The camera continues to pan left, briefly losing sight of the person, before panning back to the right.</bridge><note t="9.0-10.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000009.jpg></note><bridge t="10.0-41.0">The camera pans right, past the person in the red plaid shirt, and continues across the room. It tilts up to show the industrial-style ceiling with exposed pipes and light fixtures, then pans down across a row of large windows and a couch. The camera then pans back up, briefly capturing a man in sunglasses, before the view becomes blurry. It then continues to pan across the ceiling and down towards the large windows again, then pans back up to scan the ceiling once more before panning left, returning to the initial view of the living room area with the scattered items on the floor.</bridge><note t="41.0-42.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000041.jpg></note><bridge t="42.0-48.0">The camera continues panning left, past a dark couch, and focuses on a white desk with a lit lamp, a globe, and a painting on the wall above it. A man in a black shirt and sunglasses enters the frame from the left.</bridge><note t="48.0-49.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000048.jpg></note><bridge t="49.0-52.0">The man approaches the desk and begins to unroll a poster that says "We Can Do It!".</bridge><note t="52.0-53.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000052.jpg></note><bridge t="53.0-56.0">He continues to hold up the "We Can Do It!" poster, then begins to flip it over.</bridge><note t="56.0-57.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000056.jpg></note><bridge t="57.0-60.0">He reveals the other side of the poster, which shows Uncle Sam and the text "I WANT YOU", before flipping it back to the "We Can Do It!" side.</bridge><bridge t="60.0-66.0">The man holds the "We Can Do It!" poster against the wall, adjusting its position, then begins to flip it over again.</bridge><note t="66.0-67.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000066.jpg></note><bridge t="67.0-73.0">After briefly showing the "I WANT YOU" side, the camera quickly moves to the man's face, then pans away from him, back towards the desk with the lamp and globe. The camera continues to pan right across the desk.</bridge><note t="73.0-74.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000073.jpg></note><bridge t="74.0-80.0">The camera pans back to the left, showing the man in sunglasses briefly, then pans away from him and the desk, moving up the wall until the view is just of the wall and ceiling.</bridge>
=== Current frames ===
<frame id="1" t="80.0-81.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000080.jpg></frame>
<frame id="2" t="81.0-82.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000081.jpg></frame>
<frame id="3" t="82.0-83.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000082.jpg></frame>
<frame id="4" t="83.0-84.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000083.jpg></frame>
<frame id="5" t="84.0-85.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000084.jpg></frame>

=== QA History ===
<empty/>
Task Instructions:
1. **State and Answer**:
   - Before writing <answer>, write a short <state> paragraph that summarizes the relevant Memory and current frames. Memory contains the video history observed so far, and the current frames are the immediate video window; both are ordered by timestamp. Use them together to understand the video and check QA History to decide whether the active question should be answered now. Memory is valid evidence for earlier events, object states, locations, and cumulative results, even when the relevant moment is no longer visible. If you think the question in QA History can be answered based on the video Memory or the current frames, write your answer in <answer>. If there is no active question, or if you think the question cannot be answered yet, keep <answer></answer> empty. Do not stay silent when Memory or the current frames already provide enough evidence to answer.
   - For multiple-choice questions, choose the option best supported by Memory and the current frames. Choose an unanswerable option only when the available video evidence is genuinely insufficient.
   - **Contextual Analysis**: Carefully examine the "QA History" log, and determine the appropriate evidence scope based on the question itself. As the video progresses, a question may require multiple updates. If the question concerns an action, state, or spatial relation currently happening, the judgment should mainly rely on the latest frames, with historical memory used only as background context. If the question concerns a previous process, object location, state change, or cumulative result, the judgment should mainly rely on the notes and bridges in Memory, supplemented by the current frames. Decide whether the current evidence is sufficient to provide a new answer, supplementary information, or a correction to a previous state.
   - **Silent Criteria**: If QA History contains no active question, or if the current frames provide no useful update for an already answered question, <answer> must remain empty.

2. **Note Anchoring Strategy**:
   - **Mandatory Initialization**: If Memory is <empty/>, the first observation tag must be a <note> for <frame id="1">.
   - **Anchoring Principle**: <note> is not used to record every minor motion, but to preserve key visual evidence that may need to be revisited later. If a frame carries information that cannot be reliably preserved by a bridge alone, a <note> should be created.
   - **QA-related Anchoring**: When there is an active question in QA History, if the current frame can directly help answer, verify, disambiguate, or correct that question, a <note> should be created. In particular, anchor representative frames showing the queried object, person, location, state, or the moments around a key event.
   - **State-change Anchoring**: When the scene, camera view, workspace layout, task phase, key object location, or visible state changes significantly, and that change may affect later understanding, a <note> should be created.
   - **Representative Selection**: If multiple frames in the current window satisfy the anchoring conditions, select only the one that best represents the current state, best supports the QA, or best preserves key visual evidence. Do not create multiple notes for minor motion or pose changes.
   - **Transcription**: When outputting a note, copy the frame id from the "Current frames" block into the `frame` attribute, and copy the time range into the `t` attribute to ensure precise anchoring.

3. **Bridge Compression & Inheritance**:
   - **Open-Tail Inheritance (CRITICAL)**: Before outputting, you must check the last observation tag in Memory. If Memory ends with <bridge t="A-B">...</bridge> and no note follows it, the first output <bridge> must inherit A as its start time and rewrite/extend that original bridge, rather than starting a new time span. If there is no current <note>, write this first bridge as <bridge t="A-C">...</bridge>, where C is the current window end. If there is a current <note>, this first bridge must end at the first current note's start time, for example <bridge t="A-note_start">...</bridge>, then output the note.
   - **Prohibit Incorrect Inheritance**: When open-tail inheritance is triggered, outputting <bridge t="B-C"> is prohibited, and creating a new bridge from the current window start time is also prohibited. This is the only case where time overlap with Memory is allowed.
   - **Gap Uniqueness**: Between any two adjacent notes, or between a note and a window boundary, maintain exactly one bridge. This bridge should merge the main observations within that time gap; do not split them into multiple consecutive bridges.
   - **Internal Alignment**: Except for open-tail inheritance, all output tags must follow chronological order and connect seamlessly in time without overlap.
   - **Content Principle**: <bridge> is used to record observable action progression, state changes, and event boundaries, so that the process between adjacent notes can be reconstructed. Descriptions should be accurate and concise; do not fill in uncertain details just to make the description seem complete.
   - **Repeated Actions**: If the current process contains repeated actions, <bridge> should try to preserve the completion boundary and cumulative change of each action. Only when an action cycle is clearly completed should it be written as completed; actions that are preparatory, ongoing, occluded, or uncertain should not be counted as completed.
   - **Detail Reliability**: Details such as tools, colors, orientations, identities, text, and object attributes should be written only when they are clearly visible or explicitly confirmed by Memory. When uncertain, use conservative wording or omit the detail directly.

Other Rules:
- No text outside XML tags. bridge t uses absolute seconds.
- Memory notes do not have a frame attribute; unexpired memory notes may contain images inside the <note> tag. Current output notes must include the frame attribute.
- Current output notes must use paired tags: <note t="..." frame="N"></note>. Do not use self-closing note tags.

Output Format:
<state>...</state>
<answer>...</answer>
<bridge t="t1-t2">...</bridge>
<note t="t2-t3" frame="N"></note>
<bridge t="t3-t4">...</bridge>
...

<state>The camera has been panning around a room, focusing on a man in sunglasses who was holding a double-sided poster. base_prompt_text:
[STREAM_AGENT]
You are a streaming video agent that maintains a real-time Interleaved Memory to track the video stream and specifically answers queries from the QA History section.
In QA History, role="q" means user question, role="a" means previous model answer.

- <note>: **Visual Anchor**. Records representative or noteworthy frames that are difficult to describe in text, providing visual evidence for complex states or future questions.
- <bridge>: **Semantic Link**. Describes the evolution between anchors, capturing changes between frames in text so that the entire process can be reconstructed even without images.
=== Few-shot Example 1 (Initialization) ===
[Input]
=== Memory ===
<empty/>
...
[Output]
<state>The video begins with the operator entering the work area and starting setup. No question is active yet.</state>

<note t="0.0-1.0" frame="1"></note>
<bridge t="1.0-5.0">The operator enters the frame and begins setting up the task area at the workbench.</bridge>

=== Few-shot Example 2 (Open-Tail Inheritance with Mid-step Note) ===

=== Current frames ===
15.0-20.0s (Around t=17 the operator picks up a red component.)
[Output]
<state>The operator has been assembling a component, and the current frames show them reaching for and inspecting a red component. No question is active yet.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="10.0-17.0">The operator finishes the base alignment and reaches for a new component.</bridge>
<note t="17.0-18.0" frame="3"></note>
<bridge t="18.0-20.0">The operator inspects the red component and aligns it with the assembly.</bridge>
(Comment: The first bridge inherits start '10.0' from Memory and ends at the note's start time. The note uses paired open/close tags with an empty body. A final bridge then fills the gap from the note's end to the step end.)

=== Few-shot Example 3 (Incremental QA Update) ===

[Output]

<answer>Walking back to the workbench with the eggplant, preparing to process it.</answer>
<bridge t="15.0-20.0">The operator closes the refrigerator door and brings the eggplant back to the counter.</bridge>

=== QA History ===

=== Current frames ===
20.0-25.0s (Action continues)
[Output]

<answer></answer>

<frame id="1" t="85.0-86.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000085.jpg></frame>

<frame id="3" t="87.0-88.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000087.jpg></frame>
<frame id="4" t="88.0-89.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000088.jpg></frame>
<frame id="5" t="89.0-90.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000089.jpg></frame>

=== QA History ===
<empty/>
Task Instructions:
1. **State and Answer**:
   - Before writing <answer>, write a short <state> paragraph that summarizes the relevant Memory and current frames. Memory contains the video history observed so far, and the current frames are the immediate video window; both are ordered by timestamp. Use them together to understand the video and check QA History to decide whether the active question should be answered now. Memory is valid evidence for earlier events, object states, locations, and cumulative results, even when the relevant moment is no longer visible. If you think the question in QA History can be answered based on the video Memory or the current frames, write your answer in <answer>. If there is no active question, or if you think the question cannot be answered yet, keep <answer></answer> empty. Do not stay silent when Memory or the current frames already provide enough evidence to answer.
   - For multiple-choice questions, choose the option best supported by Memory and the current frames. Choose an unanswerable option only when the available video evidence is genuinely insufficient.
   - **Contextual Analysis**: Carefully examine the "QA History" log, and determine the appropriate evidence scope based on the question itself. As the video progresses, a question may require multiple updates. If the question concerns an action, state, or spatial relation currently happening, the judgment should mainly rely on the latest frames, with historical memory used only as background context. If the question concerns a previous process, object location, state change, or cumulative result, the judgment should mainly rely on the notes and bridges in Memory, supplemented by the current frames. Decide whether the current evidence is sufficient to provide a new answer, supplementary information, or a correction to a previous state.
   - **Silent Criteria**: If QA History contains no active question, or if the current frames provide no useful update for an already answered question, <answer> must remain empty.

2. **Note Anchoring Strategy**:
   - **Mandatory Initialization**: If Memory is <empty/>, the first observation tag must be a <note> for <frame id="1">.
   - **Anchoring Principle**: <note> is not used to record every minor motion, but to preserve key visual evidence that may need to be revisited later. If a frame carries information that cannot be reliably preserved by a bridge alone, a <note> should be created.
   - **QA-related Anchoring**: When there is an active question in QA History, if the current frame can directly help answer, verify, disambiguate, or correct that question, a <note> should be created. In particular, anchor representative frames showing the queried object, person, location, state, or the moments around a key event.
   - **State-change Anchoring**: When the scene, camera view, workspace layout, task phase, key object location, or visible state changes significantly, and that change may affect later understanding, a <note> should be created.
   - **Representative Selection**: If multiple frames in the current window satisfy the anchoring conditions, select only the one that best represents the current state, best supports the QA, or best preserves key visual evidence. Do not create multiple notes for minor motion or pose changes.
   - **Transcription**: When outputting a note, copy the frame id from the "Current frames" block into the `frame` attribute, and copy the time range into the `t` attribute to ensure precise anchoring.

3. **Bridge Compression & Inheritance**:
   - **Open-Tail Inheritance (CRITICAL)**: Before outputting, you must check the last observation tag in Memory. If Memory ends with <bridge t="A-B">...</bridge> and no note follows it, the first output <bridge> must inherit A as its start time and rewrite/extend that original bridge, rather than starting a new time span. If there is no current <note>, write this first bridge as <bridge t="A-C">...</bridge>, where C is the current window end. If there is a current <note>, this first bridge must end at the first current note's start time, for example <bridge t="A-note_start">...</bridge>, then output the note.
   - **Prohibit Incorrect Inheritance**: When open-tail inheritance is triggered, outputting <bridge t="B-C"> is prohibited, and creating a new bridge from the current window start time is also prohibited. This is the only case where time overlap with Memory is allowed.
   - **Gap Uniqueness**: Between any two adjacent notes, or between a note and a window boundary, maintain exactly one bridge. This bridge should merge the main observations within that time gap; do not split them into multiple consecutive bridges.
   - **Internal Alignment**: Except for open-tail inheritance, all output tags must follow chronological order and connect seamlessly in time without overlap.
   - **Content Principle**: <bridge> is used to record observable action progression, state changes, and event boundaries, so that the process between adjacent notes can be reconstructed. Descriptions should be accurate and concise; do not fill in uncertain details just to make the description seem complete.
   - **Repeated Actions**: If the current process contains repeated actions, <bridge> should try to preserve the completion boundary and cumulative change of each action. Only when an action cycle is clearly completed should it be written as completed; actions that are preparatory, ongoing, occluded, or uncertain should not be counted as completed.
   - **Detail Reliability**: Details such as tools, colors, orientations, identities, text, and object attributes should be written only when they are clearly visible or explicitly confirmed by Memory. When uncertain, use conservative wording or omit the detail directly.

Other Rules:
- No text outside XML tags. bridge t uses absolute seconds.
- Memory notes do not have a frame attribute; unexpired memory notes may contain images inside the <note> tag. Current output notes must include the frame attribute.
- Current output notes must use paired tags: <note t="..." frame="N"></note>. Do not use self-closing note tags.

Output Format:
<state>...</state>
<answer>...</answer>
<bridge t="t1-t2">...</bridge>
<note t="t2-t3" frame="N"></note>
<bridge t="t3-t4">...</bridge>
...

base_prompt_text:
[STREAM_AGENT]
You are a streaming video agent that maintains a real-time Interleaved Memory to track the video stream and specifically answers queries from the QA History section.
In QA History, role="q" means user question, role="a" means previous model answer.

- <note>: **Visual Anchor**. Records representative or noteworthy frames that are difficult to describe in text, providing visual evidence for complex states or future questions.
- <bridge>: **Semantic Link**. Describes the evolution between anchors, capturing changes between frames in text so that the entire process can be reconstructed even without images.
=== Few-shot Example 1 (Initialization) ===
[Input]
=== Memory ===
<empty/>
...
[Output]
<state>The video begins with the operator entering the work area and starting setup. No question is active yet.</state>
<answer></answer>
<note t="0.0-1.0" frame="1"></note>
<bridge t="1.0-5.0">The operator enters the frame and begins setting up the task area at the workbench.</bridge>

=== Few-shot Example 2 (Open-Tail Inheritance with Mid-step Note) ===
[Input]
=== Memory ===
<bridge t="10.0-15.0">The operator is assembling a component.</bridge> (Comment: Ends with a bridge, with no note after it)
=== Current frames ===
15.0-20.0s (Around t=17 the operator picks up a red component.)
[Output]
<state>The operator has been assembling a component, and the current frames show them reaching for and inspecting a red component. No question is active yet.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="10.0-17.0">The operator finishes the base alignment and reaches for a new component.</bridge>
<note t="17.0-18.0" frame="3"></note>
<bridge t="18.0-20.0">The operator inspects the red component and aligns it with the assembly.</bridge>
(Comment: The first bridge inherits start '10.0' from Memory and ends at the note's start time. The note uses paired open/close tags with an empty body. A final bridge then fills the gap from the note's end to the step end.)

=== Few-shot Example 3 (Incremental QA Update) ===
[Input]
=== QA History ===
<qa t="12.0" role="q">What is the operator doing now?</qa>
<qa t="13.0" role="a">Retrieving an eggplant from the refrigerator.</qa>
=== Current frames ===

[Output]

<answer>Walking back to the workbench with the eggplant, preparing to process it.</answer>
<bridge t="15.0-20.0">The operator closes the refrigerator door and brings the eggplant back to the counter.</bridge>

[Input]
=== QA History ===
<qa t="12.0" role="a">Walking back to the workbench with the eggplant.</qa> (Comment: No new question was asked)
=== Current frames ===
20.0-25.0s (Action continues)
[Output]
<state>The current frames continue the already answered action without adding a new useful update.</state>
<answer></answer>
<bridge t="20.0-25.0">The operator reaches the counter and places the eggplant on the cutting board.</bridge>
(Comment: <answer> is empty because there is no new question and no important conclusion has been reached.)

<frame id="3" t="92.0-93.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000092.jpg></frame>
<frame id="4" t="93.0-94.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000093.jpg></frame>
<frame id="5" t="94.0-95.0"><image:dataset/ovo/video/12/000094.jpg></frame>

=== QA History ===
<empty/>
Task Instructions:
1. **State and Answer**:
   - Before writing <answer>, write a short <state> paragraph that summarizes the relevant Memory and current frames. Memory contains the video history observed so far, and the current frames are the immediate video window; both are ordered by timestamp. Use them together to understand the video and check QA History to decide whether the active question should be answered now. Memory is valid evidence for earlier events, object states, locations, and cumulative results, even when the relevant moment is no longer visible. If you think the question in QA History can be answered based on the video Memory or the current frames, write your answer in <answer>. If there is no active question, or if you think the question cannot be answered yet, keep <answer></answer> empty. Do not stay silent when Memory or the current frames already provide enough evidence to answer.
   - For multiple-choice questions, choose the option best supported by Memory and the current frames. Choose an unanswerable option only when the available video evidence is genuinely insufficient.
   - **Contextual Analysis**: Carefully examine the "QA History" log, and determine the appropriate evidence scope based on the question itself. As the video progresses, a question may require multiple updates. If the question concerns an action, state, or spatial relation currently happening, the judgment should mainly rely on the latest frames, with historical memory used only as background context. If the question concerns a previous process, object location, state change, or cumulative result, the judgment should mainly rely on the notes and bridges in Memory, supplemented by the current frames. Decide whether the current evidence is sufficient to provide a new answer, supplementary information, or a correction to a previous state.
   - **Silent Criteria**: If QA History contains no active question, or if the current frames provide no useful update for an already answered question, <answer> must remain empty.

2. **Note Anchoring Strategy**:
   - **Mandatory Initialization**: If Memory is <empty/>, the first observation tag must be a <note> for <frame id="1">.
   - **Anchoring Principle**: <note> is not used to record every minor motion, but to preserve key visual evidence that may need to be revisited later. If a frame carries information that cannot be reliably preserved by a bridge alone, a <note> should be created.
   - **QA-related Anchoring**: When there is an active question in QA History, if the current frame can directly help answer, verify, disambiguate, or correct that question, a <note> should be created. In particular, anchor representative frames showing the queried object, person, location, state, or the moments around a key event.
   - **State-change Anchoring**: When the scene, camera view, workspace layout, task phase, key object location, or visible state changes significantly, and that change may affect later understanding, a <note> should be created.
   - **Representative Selection**: If multiple frames in the current window satisfy the anchoring conditions, select only the one that best represents the current state, best supports the QA, or best preserves key visual evidence. Do not create multiple notes for minor motion or pose changes.
   - **Transcription**: When outputting a note, copy the frame id from the "Current frames" block into the `frame` attribute, and copy the time range into the `t` attribute to ensure precise anchoring.

3. **Bridge Compression & Inheritance**:
   - **Open-Tail Inheritance (CRITICAL)**: Before outputting, you must check the last observation tag in Memory. If Memory ends with <bridge t="A-B">...</bridge> and no note follows it, the first output <bridge> must inherit A as its start time and rewrite/extend that original bridge, rather than starting a new time span. If there is no current <note>, write this first bridge as <bridge t="A-C">...</bridge>, where C is the current window end. If there is a current <note>, this first bridge must end at the first current note's start time, for example <bridge t="A-note_start">...</bridge>, then output the note.
   - **Prohibit Incorrect Inheritance**: When open-tail inheritance is triggered, outputting <bridge t="B-C"> is prohibited, and creating a new bridge from the current window start time is also prohibited. This is the only case where time overlap with Memory is allowed.
   - **Gap Uniqueness**: Between any two adjacent notes, or between a note and a window boundary, maintain exactly one bridge. This bridge should merge the main observations within that time gap; do not split them into multiple consecutive bridges.
   - **Internal Alignment**: Except for open-tail inheritance, all output tags must follow chronological order and connect seamlessly in time without overlap.
   - **Content Principle**: <bridge> is used to record observable action progression, state changes, and event boundaries, so that the process between adjacent notes can be reconstructed. Descriptions should be accurate and concise; do not fill in uncertain details just to make the description seem complete.
   - **Repeated Actions**: If the current process contains repeated actions, <bridge> should try to preserve the completion boundary and cumulative change of each action. Only when an action cycle is clearly completed should it be written as completed; actions that are preparatory, ongoing, occluded, or uncertain should not be counted as completed.
   - **Detail Reliability**: Details such as tools, colors, orientations, identities, text, and object attributes should be written only when they are clearly visible or explicitly confirmed by Memory. When uncertain, use conservative wording or omit the detail directly.

Other Rules:
- No text outside XML tags. bridge t uses absolute seconds.
- Memory notes do not have a frame attribute; unexpired memory notes may contain images inside the <note> tag. Current output notes must include the frame attribute.
- Current output notes must use paired tags: <note t="..." frame="N"></note>. Do not use self-closing note tags.

Output Format:
<state>...</state>
<answer>...</answer>
<bridge t="t1-t2">...</bridge>
<note t="t2-t3" frame="N"></note>
<bridge t="t3-t4">...</bridge>
...
